english: I. sbst. 1. resp. tsems (tshems), tooth, stéṅ-so (steng so) upper tooth; _óg-so ('og so), má-so (ma so) lower tooth; mdún-so (mdun so) Sch. gdaṅ-so (gdang so), Stg. so-drúṅ (so drung) fore-tooth, front-tooth; sbúbs-so (sbubs so), grám-so (gram so), raṅ-tag-so (rang thag so) Sch. ldaṅ-so (ldang so) cheek-tooth, molar-tooth, grinder, mčé-so (mche so) Cs., *čód-ten-so* (*chod rten so*) W. eye-tooth, corner-tooth, canine-tooth. — 2. tooth of a saw, wheel, comb. — 3. edge of a knife. II. sbst. for sa (sa), in conjunction with certain words, e.g. ṅan-sor skye-ba (ngan sor skye ba) to be born in an inferior place Mil.; v. also ráṅ-so (rang so), sór-bźag-pa (sor bzhag pa), _k̃rúl-so ('khrul so). III. sbst. joy (?), so bsod-pa (so bsod pa) id. Cs. and Lex.; cf. ṅó-so (ngo so). IV. sbst., also bso (bso), look-out, guard, spying, so byéḍ-pa (so byed pa) to spy, to look out; só-k̇uṅ (so khung) peep-hole; só-pa (so pa) keeper, guard, watchman, spy, emissary, zas nor bdúḍ-kyi só-pa yin (zas nor bdud kyi so pa yin) money and dainties are the devil's emissaries Mil.; mé-bso (me bso) a guard or watch kept by several persons round a fire; só-sgra (so sgra) 1. watchword, = sgar-míṅ (sgar ming). 2. v. comp. V. grammatical termination: *tén - so* (*rten so*) provinc. for rtén-no (rten no) G., also Glr. VI. num. for súm-ču (sum bcu) in the abbreviated numbers 31—39. VII. num.: 148. Comp. so-grí (so gri) a saw. — só-sgra (so sgra) the whistling through the teeth, in the magic performances of the Bonpo, só-sgra _débs-pa (so sgra 'debs pa) Glr. — so-čág (so chag) a broken tooth. — so-drég (so dreg) tartar? Med. — so-búd (so bud) a tooth that has come out. — so-máṅ (so mang) comb. — so-żó (so zho) a small white spot on a tooth, cf. séṅ-żo (seng zho). — so-żéḍ (so zhed) tooth-brush. — so-śíṅ (so shing) toothpick. — so-srúb (so srub) gap in the teeth Sch.

agriculture, husbandry, so-náms byéd-pa (so nams byed pa) to till the ground, to practise agriculture, farming, sgrúb-pa (sgrub pa), _báḍ-pa ('bad pa) id., so-nám-pa (so nam pa) Cs. husbandman, farmer.
Cs. a kind of berry, beneficial to the teeth.
brick, tile; also collective noun, brickwork, tiling.
coarse, thick-shelled barley, used for fodder.

1. sbst. Ssk. (prop. a climbing plant the juice of which was offered in libations to the gods and was also worshipped itself, on account of its intoxicating qualities, hence): hemp, also ytsó-ma (gtso ma), btsó-ma (btso ma); so-ma-rá-dza (so ma ra dza) id., so-ma-rá-dzai ras (so ma ra dzai ras) hemp-linen Schr., so-ma-rá-dzai tág-pa (so ma ra dzai thag pa) hempen rope Pth. — 2. adj. new, fresh, esp. W. *só-me náṅ-na zer gos* (*so me nang na zer gos*) this ought to have been mentioned directly (when it was still fresh in every body's memory).
house-keeping, management of domestic concerns, husbandry, cf. so-nam (so nam) agriculture.
lees of liquors, yeast of beer Sch.

distinct, separate, singly, individually, zas so-sói lág-tu _óṅs-so (zas so soi lag tu 'ongs so) the victuals came into the hands of the individual persons Dzl.; so-só-nas (so so nas) adv. frq.: so-só-nas snod bzéd-de (so so nas snod bzed de) 'singulatim', each for himself, holding forth his vessel Dzl.; various e.g. *na só-só* (*na so so*) W. for sna-tsogs (sna tshogs) of B.; diverse, different sám-pa so-só (bsam pa so so) different opinions, a dissension; separate, distinct, so-só byéd-pa (so so byed pa), W. *so-só čó-če* (*so so bco ce*) to separate, disjoin, divide, so-sór bźág-pa (so sor bzhag pa), W. *so-só bór-če* (*so so 'bor ce*) to set, put, lay apart. — so-soi skye-bo (so soi skye bo), prop. one separated (from the saints), one outside the pale, a man of the lower classes, of low caste; with Buddhists: a layman, and as to his spiritual condition: a man in his natural state, one not yet enlightened (like ψυχικός I Cor. 2, 14, though on account of its derivation, the above term cannot well be used for the Greek word); also the lower classes of clerical persons, monks. — so-só(s)-tar-pa (so so(s) thar pa), so-tár (so thar),
